title: Workbook — Human Sexuality
html:
Student Workbook

For use after class or in groups. Print this page or work in your notebook.

Recalling the essentials

  1. Why does the Church say that sexuality is a «gift from God» and not merely a biological function? What are the two inseparable dimensions of conjugal love?
  2. Define chastity in your own words. Why is it not «repression» but «integration»? Who is called to live chastity?
  3. How does Jesus deepen the Sixth Commandment in Matthew 5:27-28? What does this mean for the battle against lust in the heart?
  4. What are the four characteristics of Christian conjugal love (the «four marks» of marriage)? Explain each briefly.
  5. Why is pornography a grave offense against chastity? What damage does it do to the person who consumes it and to those depicted?

For reflection and writing

Answer each question sincerely. These are personal questions between you and God. There are no «correct» answers here, only honest ones.

1. «The body — and it alone — is capable of making visible what is invisible: the spiritual and the divine.» (John Paul II, Theology of the Body). What does your body — how you treat it, how you present it, how you use it — say about how you understand love? What «language» is it speaking?

Be concrete. Think about the way you dress, the images you consume, the way you touch or are touched. Is your body speaking the truth about love, or is it lying?

___________ ___________ ___________

2. Pornography is not harmless entertainment. It rewires the brain, dehumanizes the people depicted, and trains the heart to treat others as objects. Have you struggled with pornography? If so, what has it stolen from you — peace, purity, real relationships, self-respect?

If you have never struggled with it, think about the people you know who do. How does a pornified culture shape their expectations of love, their view of women and men, their capacity for real intimacy?

3. Chastity is about integration, not repression. Think of a time when you said «no» to a disordered desire — not out of fear or shame, but because you wanted something better. What was it like? What did you discover on the other side of that «no»?

If you've never done this: what desire would be the hardest to say «no» to? What keeps you from trying?

4. The Church teaches that persons with same-sex attraction must be accepted with respect, compassion, and sensitivity, and that every sign of unjust discrimination must be avoided (CCC 2358). At the same time, the Church calls them — like all the baptized — to chastity. How do you balance truth and love when discussing this with someone who experiences same-sex attraction? Have you ever been uncharitable toward someone on this topic?

This is perhaps the most difficult pastoral challenge of our time. It requires holding together two things the world wants to separate: unconditional love for the person and fidelity to God's design for sexuality. Where do you tend to lean — toward judgment without mercy, or toward affirmation without truth?

5. If you are called to marriage: what kind of spouse do you want to be? What are you doing now — today, this week — to become that person? If you are called to celibacy or consecrated life: how does your chastity bear witness to the Kingdom in a world obsessed with sex?

Your vocation begins now, not on your wedding day or ordination day. The habits you form today — purity or impurity, self-gift or self-gratification — are the foundation of your future.

Practical activities

A. A Theology of the Body audit

This week, do an honest audit of the media you consume. For three days, keep a log of every image, video, song, show, or website that has sexual content. After three days, answer these questions:

  • How many hours did you spend consuming sexualized content?
  • How did it affect your thoughts, your prayer, the way you looked at other people?
  • What would change if you fasted from all of it for a week?
  • Pick one thing to eliminate permanently. Write it here: ___________

B. Write a letter to your future spouse (or to Christ)

If you are called to marriage: write a letter to your future husband or wife. Tell them what you are doing now to protect your heart and body for them. Tell them you are praying for them. Tell them you are sorry for the ways you have already given away pieces of yourself that belonged to them. Seal it and keep it. If you are called to celibacy or religious life: write a letter to Christ, your spouse. Ask for the grace of a chaste heart, totally His.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

C. The «custody of the eyes» challenge

The desert fathers practiced «custody of the eyes» — the discipline of controlling what they looked at. For one day this week, practice it deliberately: when you see someone attractive, instead of a second look, say a quick prayer for them («Lord, bless this person, your beloved child»). When an impure image appears on your screen, close it immediately. When curiosity pulls you toward gossip about someone's relationships or body, change the subject or stay silent. At the end of the day, write down what you noticed:

 

 

 

 

Verse to memorize

«Do you not know that your body is a temple of the Holy Spirit within you, whom you have from God? You are not your own, for you were bought with a price. So glorify God in your body.»

— 1 Corinthians 6:19-20

Prayer for this week

Lord Jesus, you who are the model of every chaste heart,
teach me to see my body as you see it: a temple, not a playground.
Give me the courage to say «no» to what degrades me
and «yes» to what makes me truly free.
For every time I have treated another person as an object, forgive me.
For every time I have given away what belonged only to love, heal me.
If I am called to marriage, prepare me now to be a spouse who gives everything.
If I am called to celibacy for the Kingdom, make my heart spacious enough for all.
Blessed Chiara Badano, you who found joy even in cancer,
pray for me, that I may learn to say with you:
«For you, Jesus. If you want it, I want it too.»
Amen.

Print Workbook
